name.text: Midterm 2 - Q5V1: EM Waves + Link Budget (Ground-Penetrating Radar (GPR) through soil)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q5V1: EM Wave Propagation + Link Budget (Ground-Penetrating Radar (GPR) through soil)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 12 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Ulaby Ch.7 Example 7-5, Ch.9 Example 9-4, Problem 9.25.

Scenario

A ground-penetrating radar (GPR) system operates at \(f = 100\;\text{MHz}\). The radar signal must penetrate \(d = 2\;\text{m}\) of moist soil (\(\sigma = 0.01\;\text{S/m}\), \(\varepsilon_r = 10\), \(\mu_r = 1\)) before reflecting off a buried pipe. After emerging from the soil, the return signal travels \(R = 15\;\text{m}\) through free space to the receiver.

System Parameters

  • Transmit power: \(P_t = {@Pt_dBm@}\;\text{dBm}\)
  • Transmit antenna gain: \(G_t = {@Gt_dBi@}\;\text{dBi}\)
  • Receive antenna gain: \(G_r = {@Gr_dBi@}\;\text{dBi}\)

Part (a) — Media Classification (1 point)

Compute the loss tangent \(\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)\) and classify the material:

[[input:ans1]]   [[validation:ans1]]

Part (b) — Material Attenuation (2 points)

Calculate the attenuation constant \(\alpha\) (Np/m) and the total power attenuation (in dB) through the material layer.

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{Np/m}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 12.3

Power attenuation = \(8.686 \times \alpha \times d = \) [[input:ans3]] \(\text{dB}\)   [[validation:ans3]]

Enter a number, e.g. 45.6

Part (c) — Free-Space Path Loss (1 point)

Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) in dB for the air path.

\(\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right) = \) [[input:ans4]] \(\text{dB}\)   [[validation:ans4]]

Enter a number, e.g. 45.6

Part (d) — Link Budget (1 point)

Calculate the received power \(P_r\) in dBm:

\(P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} - \text{Material attenuation} = \) [[input:ans5]] \(\text{dBm}\)   [[validation:ans5]]

Enter a number, e.g. -23.4

Part (e) — System Improvement (2 points)

The received power is insufficient. To improve the signal by at least 6 dB, select ALL changes that would achieve this:

[[input:ans6]]   [[validation:ans6]]

Part (f) — Frequency Trade-off Analysis (5 points, manually graded)

A colleague suggests moving to a frequency 10 times higher to get more bandwidth.

Analyze the trade-offs:

  1. How would this affect the material attenuation \(\alpha\)?
  2. How would this affect the free-space path loss?
  3. How would this affect the antenna size?
  4. What is the net effect on the total link margin?
  5. Would you recommend the frequency change? Justify your answer.

[[input:ans7]]   [[validation:ans7]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V1

Part (a): Loss tangent = {@float(loss_tangent)@}. Classification: Quasi-conductor (lossy dielectric).

Part (b): \(\alpha = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{Np/m}\), Attenuation = {@ta_atten@} dB.

Part (c): FSPL = {@ta_FSPL@} dB.

Part (d): \(P_r = {@ta_Pr@}\;\text{dBm}\).

Part (e): Correct selections: {@correct_mcq@}.

Part (f): At 10x frequency: \(\alpha\) increases (more loss in material), FSPL increases by 20 dB (\(20\log_{10}(10) = 20\)), antenna becomes smaller. Net: significantly worse link margin. Generally NOT recommended unless bandwidth is critical.

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Partially correct.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Pr = Pt + Gt + Gr - FSPL - Material attenuation.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q5V2: EM Waves + Link Budget (Through-wall Wi-Fi communication)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q5V2: EM Wave Propagation + Link Budget (Through-wall Wi-Fi communication)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 12 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Ulaby Ch.7 Example 7-5, Ch.9 Example 9-4, Problem 9.25.

Scenario

A 5 GHz Wi-Fi access point transmits through a dry concrete wall (\(\sigma = 0.005\;\text{S/m}\), \(\varepsilon_r = 6\), \(\mu_r = 1\), thickness \(d = 0.3\;\text{m}\)) to a laptop \(R = 10\;\text{m}\) away (measured from the wall exit to the laptop).

System Parameters

  • Transmit power: \(P_t = {@Pt_dBm@}\;\text{dBm}\)
  • Transmit antenna gain: \(G_t = {@Gt_dBi@}\;\text{dBi}\)
  • Receive antenna gain: \(G_r = {@Gr_dBi@}\;\text{dBi}\)

Part (a) — Media Classification (1 point)

Compute the loss tangent \(\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)\) and classify the material:

[[input:ans1]]   [[validation:ans1]]

Part (b) — Material Attenuation (2 points)

Calculate the attenuation constant \(\alpha\) (Np/m) and the total power attenuation (in dB) through the material layer.

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{Np/m}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 12.3

Power attenuation = \(8.686 \times \alpha \times d = \) [[input:ans3]] \(\text{dB}\)   [[validation:ans3]]

Enter a number, e.g. 45.6

Part (c) — Free-Space Path Loss (1 point)

Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) in dB for the air path.

\(\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right) = \) [[input:ans4]] \(\text{dB}\)   [[validation:ans4]]

Enter a number, e.g. 45.6

Part (d) — Link Budget (1 point)

Calculate the received power \(P_r\) in dBm:

\(P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} - \text{Material attenuation} = \) [[input:ans5]] \(\text{dBm}\)   [[validation:ans5]]

Enter a number, e.g. -23.4

Part (e) — System Improvement (2 points)

The received power is insufficient. To improve the signal by at least 6 dB, select ALL changes that would achieve this:

[[input:ans6]]   [[validation:ans6]]

Part (f) — Frequency Trade-off Analysis (5 points, manually graded)

A colleague suggests moving to a frequency 10 times higher to get more bandwidth.

Analyze the trade-offs:

  1. How would this affect the material attenuation \(\alpha\)?
  2. How would this affect the free-space path loss?
  3. How would this affect the antenna size?
  4. What is the net effect on the total link margin?
  5. Would you recommend the frequency change? Justify your answer.

[[input:ans7]]   [[validation:ans7]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V2

Part (a): Loss tangent = {@float(loss_tangent)@}. Classification: Quasi-conductor (lossy dielectric).

Part (b): \(\alpha = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{Np/m}\), Attenuation = {@ta_atten@} dB.

Part (c): FSPL = {@ta_FSPL@} dB.

Part (d): \(P_r = {@ta_Pr@}\;\text{dBm}\).

Part (e): Correct selections: {@correct_mcq@}.

Part (f): At 10x frequency: \(\alpha\) increases (more loss in material), FSPL increases by 20 dB (\(20\log_{10}(10) = 20\)), antenna becomes smaller. Net: significantly worse link margin. Generally NOT recommended unless bandwidth is critical.

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Partially correct.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Pr = Pt + Gt + Gr - FSPL - Material attenuation.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q5V3: EM Waves + Link Budget (Agricultural IoT sensor through wet earth)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q5V3: EM Wave Propagation + Link Budget (Agricultural IoT sensor through wet earth)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 12 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Ulaby Ch.7 Example 7-5, Ch.9 Example 9-4, Problem 9.25.

Scenario

An agricultural IoT sensor buried at depth \(d = 0.5\;\text{m}\) in moist soil (\(\sigma = 0.02\;\text{S/m}\), \(\varepsilon_r = 15\), \(\mu_r = 1\)) transmits at \(f = 433\;\text{MHz}\) (ISM band). After emerging from the soil, the signal travels \(R = 300\;\text{m}\) through free space to a gateway receiver.

System Parameters

  • Transmit power: \(P_t = {@Pt_dBm@}\;\text{dBm}\)
  • Transmit antenna gain: \(G_t = {@Gt_dBi@}\;\text{dBi}\)
  • Receive antenna gain: \(G_r = {@Gr_dBi@}\;\text{dBi}\)

Part (a) — Media Classification (1 point)

Compute the loss tangent \(\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)\) and classify the material:

[[input:ans1]]   [[validation:ans1]]

Part (b) — Material Attenuation (2 points)

Calculate the attenuation constant \(\alpha\) (Np/m) and the total power attenuation (in dB) through the material layer.

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{Np/m}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 12.3

Power attenuation = \(8.686 \times \alpha \times d = \) [[input:ans3]] \(\text{dB}\)   [[validation:ans3]]

Enter a number, e.g. 45.6

Part (c) — Free-Space Path Loss (1 point)

Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) in dB for the air path.

\(\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right) = \) [[input:ans4]] \(\text{dB}\)   [[validation:ans4]]

Enter a number, e.g. 45.6

Part (d) — Link Budget (1 point)

Calculate the received power \(P_r\) in dBm:

\(P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} - \text{Material attenuation} = \) [[input:ans5]] \(\text{dBm}\)   [[validation:ans5]]

Enter a number, e.g. -23.4

Part (e) — System Improvement (2 points)

The received power is insufficient. To improve the signal by at least 6 dB, select ALL changes that would achieve this:

[[input:ans6]]   [[validation:ans6]]

Part (f) — Frequency Trade-off Analysis (5 points, manually graded)

A colleague suggests moving to a frequency 10 times higher to get more bandwidth.

Analyze the trade-offs:

  1. How would this affect the material attenuation \(\alpha\)?
  2. How would this affect the free-space path loss?
  3. How would this affect the antenna size?
  4. What is the net effect on the total link margin?
  5. Would you recommend the frequency change? Justify your answer.

[[input:ans7]]   [[validation:ans7]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V3

Part (a): Loss tangent = {@float(loss_tangent)@}. Classification: Quasi-conductor (lossy dielectric).

Part (b): \(\alpha = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{Np/m}\), Attenuation = {@ta_atten@} dB.

Part (c): FSPL = {@ta_FSPL@} dB.

Part (d): \(P_r = {@ta_Pr@}\;\text{dBm}\).

Part (e): Correct selections: {@correct_mcq@}.

Part (f): At 10x frequency: \(\alpha\) increases (more loss in material), FSPL increases by 20 dB (\(20\log_{10}(10) = 20\)), antenna becomes smaller. Net: significantly worse link margin. Generally NOT recommended unless bandwidth is critical.

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Partially correct.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Pr = Pt + Gt + Gr - FSPL - Material attenuation.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q5V4: EM Waves + Link Budget (Satellite-to-indoor receiver through roof)

questiontext.text:
Midterm 2 – Q5V4: EM Wave Propagation + Link Budget (Satellite-to-indoor receiver through roof)

Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

Total: 12 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 10%

Inspired by Ulaby Ch.7 Example 7-5, Ch.9 Example 9-4, Problem 9.25.

Scenario

A satellite downlink at \(f = 12\;\text{GHz}\) passes through a building roof (reinforced concrete: \(\sigma = 0.1\;\text{S/m}\), \(\varepsilon_r = 7\), \(\mu_r = 1\), thickness \(d = 0.2\;\text{m}\)). The satellite is \(R = 500\;\text{m}\) slant range from the building.

System Parameters

  • Transmit power: \(P_t = {@Pt_dBm@}\;\text{dBm}\)
  • Transmit antenna gain: \(G_t = {@Gt_dBi@}\;\text{dBi}\)
  • Receive antenna gain: \(G_r = {@Gr_dBi@}\;\text{dBi}\)

Part (a) — Media Classification (1 point)

Compute the loss tangent \(\tan\delta = \sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)\) and classify the material:

[[input:ans1]]   [[validation:ans1]]

Part (b) — Material Attenuation (2 points)

Calculate the attenuation constant \(\alpha\) (Np/m) and the total power attenuation (in dB) through the material layer.

\(\alpha = \) [[input:ans2]] \(\text{Np/m}\)   [[validation:ans2]]

Enter a number, e.g. 12.3

Power attenuation = \(8.686 \times \alpha \times d = \) [[input:ans3]] \(\text{dB}\)   [[validation:ans3]]

Enter a number, e.g. 45.6

Part (c) — Free-Space Path Loss (1 point)

Calculate the free-space path loss (FSPL) in dB for the air path.

\(\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right) = \) [[input:ans4]] \(\text{dB}\)   [[validation:ans4]]

Enter a number, e.g. 45.6

Part (d) — Link Budget (1 point)

Calculate the received power \(P_r\) in dBm:

\(P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} - \text{Material attenuation} = \) [[input:ans5]] \(\text{dBm}\)   [[validation:ans5]]

Enter a number, e.g. -23.4

Part (e) — System Improvement (2 points)

The received power is insufficient. To improve the signal by at least 6 dB, select ALL changes that would achieve this:

[[input:ans6]]   [[validation:ans6]]

Part (f) — Frequency Trade-off Analysis (5 points, manually graded)

A colleague suggests moving to a frequency 10 times higher to get more bandwidth.

Analyze the trade-offs:

  1. How would this affect the material attenuation \(\alpha\)?
  2. How would this affect the free-space path loss?
  3. How would this affect the antenna size?
  4. What is the net effect on the total link margin?
  5. Would you recommend the frequency change? Justify your answer.

[[input:ans7]]   [[validation:ans7]]

generalfeedback.text:
Solution for V4

Part (a): Loss tangent = {@float(loss_tangent)@}. Classification: Quasi-conductor (lossy dielectric).

Part (b): \(\alpha = {@ta_alpha@}\;\text{Np/m}\), Attenuation = {@ta_atten@} dB.

Part (c): FSPL = {@ta_FSPL@} dB.

Part (d): \(P_r = {@ta_Pr@}\;\text{dBm}\).

Part (e): Correct selections: {@correct_mcq@}.

Part (f): At 10x frequency: \(\alpha\) increases (more loss in material), FSPL increases by 20 dB (\(20\log_{10}(10) = 20\)), antenna becomes smaller. Net: significantly worse link margin. Generally NOT recommended unless bandwidth is critical.

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Partially correct.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Pr = Pt + Gt + Gr - FSPL - Material attenuation.

name.text: Midterm 2 - Q5: Upload Handwritten Work (EM Waves + Link Budget)

questiontext.text:
Upload Handwritten Work for Q5

Upload work for Question 5 (EM Wave Propagation + Wireless Link Budget).

Include:

  • Loss tangent calculation and classification
  • Alpha computation with formula for the medium type
  • FSPL calculation
  • Complete link budget arithmetic
  • Frequency trade-off analysis for Part (f)

Up to 2 files. Worth 0 points.

Teacher notice: Set Allow attachments to 2+ in quiz settings.

graderinfo.text:
• Check loss tangent and classification
  • Verify alpha formula matches medium type
  • For Part (f): look for multi-dimensional trade-off analysis, not just one factor
